perturbation.original_text: 9. All right, title and interest in and to a Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information will remain the exclusive property of the Disclosing Party and the Confidential Information will be held in trust and confidence by the Recipient and its Representatives. No interest, license or any right respecting the Confidential Information, other than expressly set out in this Agreement, is granted to the Recipient under this Agreement by implication or otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. All right, title and interest in and to a Disclosing Party’s Secret Information will remain the exclusive property of the Disclosing Party and the Confidential Information will be held in trust and confidence by the Recipient and its Representatives. No interest, license or any right respecting the Confidential Information, other than expressly set out in this Agreement, is granted to the Recipient under this Agreement by implication or otherwise.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing 'Confidential Information' with 'Secret Information' at the beginning of Section 9, but retaining 'Confidential Information' later in the same section, this creates an in-text contradiction. It becomes unclear if the right, title, and interest belong to the 'Secret Information' or the 'Confidential Information', creating uncertainty regarding ownership.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 9. All right, title and interest in and to a Disclosing Party’s Secret Information will remain the exclusive property of the Disclosing Party and the Confidential Information will be held in trust and confidence by the Recipient and its Representatives. No interest, license or any right respecting the Confidential Information, other than expressly set out in this Agreement, is granted to the Recipient under this Agreement by implication or otherwise.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'Confidential Information' with 'Secret Information' at the beginning of Section 9, while retaining 'Confidential Information' later in the same section. This creates an in-text contradiction because it is unclear to which 'information' the right, title, and interest belong, or the right to 'hold in trust' applies. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology' definition because the contract uses multiple terms without defining them or delineating their relationships, leading to conflicting obligations and creating a direct conflict within the document. The changed text and contradicted text are in the same location, creating a direct contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 11. The Recipient will, within five business days of a written request by the Disclosing Party, confirm that it has destroyed the original provided to Recipient (and all copies made by it) any documents (in whatever Project Bell – Non-disclosure Agreement Page 3 of 6 form or medium) provided, or made available for review by, the Disclosing Party (including any Derivative Confidential Information). Notwithstanding the immediately preceding sentence, the parties agree that destruction will not be made of (i) any electronic copy of any Confidential Information that was created pursuant to the standard electronic backup and archival procedures of the Recipient if (x) personnel of the Recipient whose functions are not primarily internal information technology in nature do not have access to such retained copies and (y) personnel of the Recipient whose functions are primarily internal information technology in nature have access to such copies only as reasonably necessary for the performance of their internal information technology duties ( e.g. , for purposes of system recovery). The parties further agree that they will continue to treat as confidential in accordance with the Agreement, any Confidential Information retained pursuant to the second sentence of this Section 11.
perturbation.changed_text: 11. The Recipient will, within five business days of a written request by the Disclosing Party, confirm that it has destroyed the original provided to Recipient (and all copies made by it) any documents (in whatever Project Bell – Non-disclosure Agreement Page 3 of 6 form or medium) provided, or made available for review by, the Disclosing Party (including any Derivative Sensitive Information). Notwithstanding the immediately preceding sentence, the parties agree that destruction will not be made of (i) any electronic copy of any Confidential Information that was created pursuant to the standard electronic backup and archival procedures of the Recipient if (x) personnel of the Recipient whose functions are not primarily internal information technology in nature do not have access to such retained copies and (y) personnel of the Recipient whose functions are primarily internal information technology in nature have access to such copies only as reasonably necessary for the performance of their internal information technology duties ( e.g. , for purposes of system recovery). The parties further agree that they will continue to treat as confidential in accordance with the Agreement, any Confidential Information retained pursuant to the second sentence of this Section 11.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Derivative Confidential Information' to 'Derivative Sensitive Information' at the beginning of Section 11, while keeping 'Confidential Information' throughout the rest of the section, creates an in-text contradiction. This raises the question whether the destruction requirement only applies to 'Derivative Sensitive Information', or if the exception for electronic copies applies to all 'Confidential Information' even if it's not deemed sensitive. The term 'Sensitive Information' does not exist in the original text and that create a high level of uncertanity, therefore an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 11. The Recipient will, within five business days of a written request by the Disclosing Party, confirm that it has destroyed the original provided to Recipient (and all copies made by it) any documents (in whatever Project Bell – Non-disclosure Agreement Page 3 of 6 form or medium) provided, or made available for review by, the Disclosing Party (including any Derivative Sensitive Information). Notwithstanding the immediately preceding sentence, the parties agree that destruction will not be made of (i) any electronic copy of any Confidential Information that was created pursuant to the standard electronic backup and archival procedures of the Recipient if (x) personnel of the Recipient whose functions are not primarily internal information technology in nature do not have access to such retained copies and (y) personnel of the Recipient whose functions are primarily internal information technology in nature have access to such copies only as reasonably necessary for the performance of their internal information technology duties ( e.g. , for purposes of system recovery). The parties further agree that they will continue to treat as confidential in accordance with the Agreement, any Confidential Information retained pursuant to the second sentence of this Section 11.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'Derivative Confidential Information' with 'Derivative Sensitive Information' in Section 11, while the rest of the section refers to 'Confidential Information.' The contradicted text uses 'Derivative Sensitive Information' at the beginning but continues with 'Confidential Information' afterwards. This creates a contradiction because it is unclear whether the destruction requirement and the exception for electronic copies apply only to 'Derivative Sensitive Information' or to all 'Confidential Information,' leading to uncertainty and a misalignment of terminology as described in the perturbation explanation. Because 'Sensitive Information' is not defined, and it replaces part of a phrase that is also used in the unchanged rest of the contract, this creates a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 15. The furnishing of Confidential Information pursuant to this Agreement does not obligate either party to enter into any further agreement or negotiation with the other or to refrain from entering into any agreement or negotiation with another party with respect to a Proposed Transaction or otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: 15. The furnishing of Protected Data pursuant to this Agreement does not obligate either party to enter into any further agreement or negotiation with the other or to refrain from entering into any agreement or negotiation with another party with respect to a Proposed Transaction or otherwise.
perturbation.explanation: In section 15, changing 'Confidential Information' to 'Protected Data' creates an in-text contradiction. While 'Confidential Information' is defined in Section 1, 'Protected Data' is not defined anywhere in the document. This ambiguity introduces uncertainty regarding the scope of what information is protected under this clause, particularly in the context of further agreements or negotiations.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 15. The furnishing of Protected Data pursuant to this Agreement does not obligate either party to enter into any further agreement or negotiation with the other or to refrain from entering into any agreement or negotiation with another party with respect to a Proposed Transaction or otherwise.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Confidential Information' to 'Protected Data' in section 15 creates an in-text contradiction. 'Confidential Information' is defined in Section 1, whereas 'Protected Data' is not defined anywhere in the document. This introduces ambiguity. The explanation states that the lack of definition for 'Protected Data' creates uncertainty and the 'Misaligned Terminology' perturbation type states that this can lead to in-text contradictions when the contract uses multiple terms interchangeably without defining them.

perturbation.original_text: 20. To the extent that any Confidential Information includes materials subject to the attorney-client privilege or similar privileges or protections, such disclosure is inadvertent and the Disclosing Party is not waiving, and shall not be deemed to have waived or diminished, its attorney work product protections, attorney-client privileges or similar protections and privileges as a result of disclosing any Confidential Information (including Confidential Information related to pending or threatened litigation) to the Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 20. To the extent that any Private Data includes materials subject to the attorney-client privilege or similar privileges or protections, such disclosure is inadvertent and the Disclosing Party is not waiving, and shall not be deemed to have waived or diminished, its attorney work product protections, attorney-client privileges or similar protections and privileges as a result of disclosing any Confidential Information (including Confidential Information related to pending or threatened litigation) to the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: Section 20 now refers to 'Private Data' at the start but then reverts to 'Confidential Information'. Because 'Private Data' is never formally defined, it creates confusion as to whether the waiver provisions apply only to a subset of information considered 'Private Data', or all information. This also undermines the definition provided in Section 1 for the term 'Confidential Information'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 20. To the extent that any Private Data includes materials subject to the attorney-client privilege or similar privileges or protections, such disclosure is inadvertent and the Disclosing Party is not waiving, and shall not be deemed to have waived or diminished, its attorney work product protections, attorney-client privileges or similar protections and privileges as a result of disclosing any Confidential Information (including Confidential Information related to pending or threatened litigation) to the Receiving Party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces 'Private Data' at the beginning of section 20, but then uses 'Confidential Information' later in the same section. The original text only uses the term 'Confidential Information'. The perturbation explanation indicates that 'Private Data' is never formally defined, creating confusion and contradicting the definition of 'Confidential Information'. Since the contradicted text also refers to 'Private Data', the inconsistent use of 'Private Data' and 'Confidential Information' creates a direct conflict, undermining the intended meaning and enforceability of the clause, which aligns with the Misaligned Terminology definition.
